transcript.speaker: b
transcript.text: The Buddhist school's inability to counter our claims only serves to further establish the validity of the Advaita Vedanta position on atman. As we have previously stated, the atman is not only distinct from karma but also its cause, as it is the atman that ultimately experiences the fruits of karma (cite: edge_018922). Furthermore, the atman's qualified aspect of dharma is evident in its association with the values of long life, wealth, and holiness, which are considered important in the pursuit of a virtuous life (cite: edge_019087). The atman's distinctness and eternity are fundamental to our school's understanding of the ultimate reality, and we maintain that it is the unchanging and eternal essence that underlies all existence (cite: edge_018915).

verdict: This debate is largely one-sided, with the Advaita Vedanta school (B) presenting a clear and consistent position on the concept of "atman" with strong textual support. In contrast, the Buddhist school (A) fails to provide any valid premises to support their claim that the atman is empty of inherent existence, effectively conceding the point. The core point of disagreement lies in the existence and nature of the atman, with Advaita Vedanta positing a real, unchanging, and eternal entity, while the Buddhist school is unable to counter this claim due to a lack of cited premises. Overall, the Advaita Vedanta school has stronger textual support given the premises actually cited.
